---
title: "Chapter 15 — ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 14 · 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals](ch14.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [16 · 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks →](ch16.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Building a deep network is the easy part; making it actually learn is where most of the difficulty lives. This chapter is about the practical machinery of optimization — how we navigate a high-dimensional loss surface, which optimizers and schedules get us there, and the tricks (normalization, dropout, residual connections, augmentation) that turn a network that diverges or memorizes into one that generalizes. It sits squarely in the Deep Learning part: the architectures come from neighboring chapters, but nothing trains itself.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep Learning · turning a defined network into one that actually converges and generalizes · the one key idea: training is gradient descent on a messy non-convex surface, and every technique here either smooths the path, stabilizes the gradients, or curbs overfitting
:::

## 15.1 — The loss landscape

Imagine the network's parameters as coordinates on a vast, hilly terrain, and the height at each point as the **loss** — how badly the model does on the training data. Training is just walking downhill. With millions of parameters this terrain has millions of dimensions, and unlike the tidy bowl of a linear model, a deep network's loss surface is **non-convex**: it has many valleys, ridges, and flat plains.

The intuition most people start with — "we might get stuck in a bad local minimum" — turns out to be mostly wrong in high dimensions. The real obstacles are **saddle points**: places where the surface curves up in some directions and down in others, like a mountain pass. At a saddle the gradient is near zero, so naive gradient descent crawls, even though there is still a way down. High-dimensional spaces are full of saddles, and most local minima that do exist turn out to have similar, low loss — so where you land matters less than getting moving and not stalling.

Two more features shape everything that follows. **Sharp** minima (narrow, steep valleys) tend to generalize worse than **flat** minima (wide basins), because a flat basin means nearby parameter settings also work — the solution is robust to small shifts between train and test. And the surface is full of **ravines**: long narrow valleys where the floor descends gently but the walls are steep. Plain gradient descent zig-zags across the walls of a ravine while barely advancing along its floor, which is exactly the pathology momentum was invented to fix.

::: {.callout-tip}
Don't fear local minima — fear saddle points and ravines. Almost every optimizer trick below is really about not stalling on flat regions and not bouncing across narrow valleys.
:::

## 15.2 — Optimizers: from SGD to AdamW

The base algorithm is **gradient descent**: nudge each parameter in the direction that most reduces the loss. Computing the gradient over the whole dataset every step is too expensive, so we use **stochastic gradient descent (SGD)** — estimate the gradient from a small random **mini-batch**. The update for parameter vector $\theta$ with learning rate $\eta$ is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, g_t, \qquad g_t = \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\text{batch})$$

The mini-batch gradient is noisy, but that noise is a feature: it helps the optimizer rattle past saddle points and shallow traps. Plain SGD's weakness is the ravine problem — it oscillates across steep walls.

**Momentum** fixes this by accumulating a velocity, like a ball rolling downhill that keeps its heading. Instead of stepping by the raw gradient, you step by an exponentially decaying average of past gradients. Oscillations across the ravine walls cancel out; consistent progress along the floor adds up.

$$v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + (1-\beta)\, g_t, \qquad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, v_t$$

with $\beta \approx 0.9$. (You will also see the "heavy ball" convention $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + g_t$, which drops the $(1-\beta)$ factor and so takes effectively larger early steps; the form above is a true exponentially decaying average and is what "EMA of gradients" literally means.) **Nesterov momentum** is a small refinement: it computes the gradient at the *look-ahead* position $\theta_t - \eta\beta v_{t-1}$ — where momentum is about to carry you — rather than where you currently stand. Peeking ahead lets it brake earlier when the slope flattens, giving slightly more stable convergence.

The next problem: one global learning rate is wrong because different parameters need different step sizes. **RMSprop** gives each parameter its own adaptive rate by dividing the step by a running average of that parameter's recent squared gradients — parameters with consistently large gradients get smaller steps, rarely-updated ones get larger steps.

$$s_t = \rho s_{t-1} + (1-\rho) g_t^2, \qquad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{s_t}+\epsilon}\, g_t$$

**Adam** is the workhorse default: it combines momentum (a running mean of gradients, $m_t$) with RMSprop's per-parameter scaling (a running mean of squared gradients, $v_t$). Both running means are initialized at zero, so early in training — before the exponential average has "warmed up" — they sit biased toward zero. Adam's **bias correction** divides each by $(1-\beta^t)$, a factor that is small (a big correction) when $t$ is small and fades to $1$ as training proceeds, so the estimates aren't dragged toward zero in the first steps.

```python
# Adam, the mechanism in plain numpy
m = np.zeros_like(theta); v = np.zeros_like(theta)
for t in range(1, steps + 1):
    g = grad(theta, batch())                    # mini-batch gradient
    m = b1 * m + (1 - b1) * g                    # 1st moment: momentum
    v = b2 * v + (1 - b2) * g**2                 # 2nd moment: per-param scale
    m_hat = m / (1 - b1**t)                      # bias-correct: m starts at 0, so undo it
    v_hat = v / (1 - b2**t)                      # (t small -> big correction, fades to 1)
    theta -= lr * m_hat / (np.sqrt(v_hat) + eps) # adaptive, momentum-aware step
# defaults that almost always work: b1=0.9, b2=0.999, eps=1e-8
```

**AdamW** is the version you should actually reach for. Standard Adam folds **weight decay** in as an L2 penalty on the gradient, but the adaptive denominator then scales that penalty unevenly across parameters, weakening regularization exactly where gradients are large. AdamW *decouples* weight decay — it shrinks weights by a fixed fraction directly in the update, separate from the gradient path — which restores clean, uniform regularization and is why AdamW is the standard for training transformers and most modern models.

| Optimizer | What it adds | Fixes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| SGD | mini-batch gradient | baseline; noisy, zig-zags in ravines |
| + Momentum | velocity (EMA of gradients) | ravine oscillation, slow saddles |
| + Nesterov | look-ahead gradient | overshoot near minima |
| RMSprop | per-param squared-grad scaling | one-size learning rate |
| Adam | momentum + RMSprop + bias correction | both, with little tuning |
| AdamW | decoupled weight decay | broken regularization in Adam |

::: {.callout-warning}
Adam is forgiving, but "forgiving" is not "free." It can converge to sharper, worse-generalizing minima than well-tuned SGD+momentum on some vision tasks, and its default `eps` and learning rate still matter. If you copied an L2 penalty into an Adam setup and regularization seems weak, you almost certainly want AdamW instead.
:::

## 15.3 — Learning-rate schedules and warmup

The single most important hyperparameter is the **learning rate** $\eta$. Too large and training diverges or oscillates; too small and it crawls and may settle in a poor spot. The trick is that the *best* learning rate changes over training — you want big steps early to cover ground, small steps late to settle precisely into a basin. A **learning-rate schedule** varies $\eta$ over time to get both.

The most common modern choice is **cosine decay**: start at a peak rate and ease down to near zero following a half-cosine curve. It spends a lot of time at a moderate rate and slows gently at the end, which empirically lands in flatter minima. Older but still useful schedules include **step decay** (cut the rate by a factor every N epochs) and **exponential decay**.

**Warmup** is the counterintuitive companion. For the first few hundred or few thousand steps, you *ramp the learning rate up* from near zero to the peak before any decay begins. Why start slow? At initialization the weights are random and the adaptive optimizer's moment estimates are garbage — a full-size step on a bad gradient can blow training apart. Warmup lets the statistics stabilize first. It is close to mandatory when training transformers, where skipping it is a classic cause of early divergence.

Putting the two together gives the standard modern curve: a short linear **warmup** up to the peak rate, then a long **cosine decay** down toward zero.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 140" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <line x1="40" y1="115" x2="340" y2="115" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="115" x2="40" y2="20" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="190" y="133" text-anchor="middle">training step →</text>
  <text x="14" y="68" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 14 68)">LR →</text>
  <!-- warmup: linear ramp from 0 up to peak at x=110 -->
  <path d="M 40 115 L 110 30" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- cosine decay: peak at (110,30) easing down to near zero at (330,112) -->
  <path d="M 110 30 C 180 30, 230 112, 330 112" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="30" x2="110" y2="115" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.6" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/>
  <text x="110" y="50" text-anchor="middle">peak</text>
  <text x="72" y="98" text-anchor="middle">warmup</text>
  <text x="235" y="70" text-anchor="middle">cosine decay</text>
</svg>

That leaves one practical question the schedule does not answer: what *is* the peak rate? The quickest actionable recipe is the **learning-rate range test** — start from a tiny rate and increase it geometrically over a few hundred steps while watching the loss; the loss falls, bottoms out, then shoots up as the rate gets too large, and you pick a peak roughly an order of magnitude below where it blew up (near the steepest part of the descent). Lacking that, just sweep powers of ten — $10^{-2}, 10^{-3}, 10^{-4}$ — and keep the largest one that trains stably. For Adam/AdamW, $3\times10^{-4}$ is a famously reliable starting guess.

## 15.4 — Normalization: batch and layer norm

As a signal passes through many layers, its scale can drift — one layer's outputs become tiny, the next layer's huge. The original motivation for normalization was to fight this moving-target distribution, named **internal covariate shift**: if every layer must constantly re-adapt to a shifting input distribution from the layer below, training slows. That story is intuitive but contested — later work (Santurkar et al.) showed normalization helps mainly by *smoothing the loss surface*, with the covariate-shift effect largely a myth — so treat internal covariate shift as the historical motivation, not the settled mechanism. Either way, the cure is to **normalize** activations back to a stable scale partway through the network.

**Batch normalization (BatchNorm)** normalizes each feature across the examples *in the current mini-batch*: subtract the batch mean, divide by the batch standard deviation, then apply two learned parameters $\gamma$ (scale) and $\beta$ (shift) so the network can undo the normalization if it needs to.

$$\hat{x} = \frac{x - \mu_{\text{batch}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{batch}} + \epsilon}}, \qquad y = \gamma \hat{x} + \beta$$

BatchNorm both speeds and stabilizes training, and its batch-to-batch noise adds a mild regularizing effect. Its catch: it depends on the batch. With tiny batches the statistics are unreliable, and it behaves differently at training time (batch stats) versus inference (running-average stats), which is a frequent source of bugs.

**Layer normalization (LayerNorm)** sidesteps this by normalizing across the *features of a single example* instead of across the batch. Because it never looks at other examples, it works identically with batch size 1, in recurrent settings, and at inference — which is exactly why it, not BatchNorm, is the norm of choice inside transformers.

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| Normalizes over | the batch, per feature | the features, per example |
| Batch-size dependent | yes (breaks at small N) | no |
| Train ≠ inference | yes (running stats) | no |
| Home turf | CNNs, large batches | Transformers, RNNs |

## 15.5 — Regularization: dropout, weight decay, augmentation, early stopping

A deep network has more than enough capacity to memorize its training set. **Regularization** is any technique that fights this **overfitting** — closing the gap between strong training performance and weak test performance.

**Dropout** is the most distinctively neural trick. During training, randomly "drop" (zero out) each neuron with probability $p$ on every forward pass. No neuron can rely on any specific partner, so the network is forced to spread its representation across redundant pathways — like a team that cross-trains so it survives anyone being out sick. The modern convention is **inverted dropout**: you rescale the surviving activations by $1/(1-p)$ *during training* so their expected magnitude is unchanged, which means at test time you do nothing — every neuron is active and no rescaling is needed because it already happened during training.

```python
def dropout(x, p, training):
    if not training:
        return x                                     # test time: do nothing
    mask = (np.random.rand(*x.shape) > p) / (1 - p)  # keep & rescale (inverted dropout)
    return x * mask          # zeroed units + survivors scaled up by 1/(1-p)
```

**Weight decay** pulls weights toward zero each step, preferring simpler, smaller-magnitude solutions. As noted in 15.2, classic **L2 regularization** (adding $\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\lVert\theta\rVert^2$ to the loss) is *identical* to weight decay only for plain SGD; with adaptive optimizers the two diverge, and decoupled weight decay (AdamW) is what you want.

**Data augmentation** attacks overfitting from the data side: synthesize new training examples by applying label-preserving transformations — flip, crop, rotate, or color-jitter an image; for text, swap synonyms or back-translate. A cat is still a cat when mirrored, so the model sees effectively more data and learns to ignore irrelevant variation.

**Early stopping** is the laziest regularizer and one of the best: watch validation loss during training and stop when it starts rising even as training loss keeps falling — that turn is the moment the model begins memorizing rather than learning. Keep the checkpoint from the lowest validation loss.

::: {.callout-warning}
Augmentations must preserve the label. Horizontally flipping a photo of a "6" can make it a "9", and flipping a stop sign or a chest X-ray can quietly destroy the very signal you need. Pick transformations that respect your task's invariances, not a generic recipe.
:::

## 15.6 — Vanishing and exploding gradients, and the fixes

Backpropagation computes gradients by multiplying many per-layer terms together via the chain rule. Multiply many numbers smaller than one and the product shrinks toward zero; multiply many larger than one and it explodes. In a deep stack this means early layers can receive **vanishing gradients** (so tiny they never learn) or **exploding gradients** (so huge that updates overshoot and the loss goes to NaN). This was the single biggest barrier to training deep networks, and the modern toolkit is essentially a set of answers to it.

- **ReLU activations.** Saturating activations like sigmoid have near-zero slope over most of their range, multiplying in vanishingly small factors. **ReLU**, $\max(0, x)$, has slope exactly 1 for positive inputs, so gradients pass through deep stacks undiminished. (Variants like LeakyReLU and GELU patch ReLU's own dead-neuron issue — see Chapter 14.)
- **Residual (skip) connections.** Instead of $y = F(x)$, a residual block computes $y = x + F(x)$. The chain rule then always has a clean "+1" path for the gradient to flow straight back, so even very deep networks (hundreds of layers) stay trainable. This is the idea that unlocked modern depth.
- **Normalization** (15.4) keeps activation scales in a sane range layer to layer, indirectly keeping gradient magnitudes well-behaved.
- **Careful initialization.** Schemes like Xavier and He scale the initial random weights so the signal's variance is preserved across layers, so gradients neither shrink nor blow up at step zero (see Chapter 14).
- **Gradient clipping.** A direct seatbelt for explosions: if the gradient's overall norm exceeds a threshold, rescale it down before stepping. Cheap, and standard in RNNs and LLM training where occasional huge gradients are common.

$$\text{if } \lVert g\rVert > \tau: \quad g \leftarrow \tau \cdot \frac{g}{\lVert g\rVert}$$

A residual connection's gradient path, visually — the gradient reaching the block's input is the sum of the path *through* $F$ and the identity shortcut, so it can never vanish entirely:

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 110" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12">
  <rect x="120" y="20" width="70" height="34" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="155" y="41" text-anchor="middle">F(x)</text>
  <circle cx="250" cy="37" r="13" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="250" y="42" text-anchor="middle">+</text>
  <line x1="40" y1="37" x2="120" y2="37" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <line x1="190" y1="37" x2="237" y2="37" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <path d="M 80 37 Q 80 90 250 90 L 250 50" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="165" y="104" text-anchor="middle">identity shortcut (the "+1" path)</text>
  <line x1="263" y1="37" x2="330" y2="37" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="30" y="41" text-anchor="middle">x</text>
  <defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="currentColor"/></marker></defs>
</svg>

## 15.7 — Batch size, epochs, and mixed precision

The **batch size** is how many examples you average a gradient over per step. Small batches give noisy gradients — slower per example but with a regularizing wander that often generalizes better. Large batches give smoother, more parallel-friendly gradients (better hardware utilization) but tend toward sharper minima and need care to match small-batch quality. The two ends trade off cleanly:

| | Small batch | Large batch |
|---|-------------|-------------|
| Gradient noise | high (regularizing wander) | low (smooth estimate) |
| Hardware use / parallelism | underuses the GPU | saturates it, fewer steps |
| Minima it favors | flatter, often generalizes better | sharper, needs care |
| Generalization | usually strong | can lag without tuning |
| Learning-rate coupling | baseline LR | scale LR up ~$k\times$ (linear scaling rule) |

A useful rule of thumb: when you scale the batch size up by $k$, scale the learning rate up by roughly $k$ too (the **linear scaling rule**), usually paired with warmup to survive the larger early steps. And when the batch you *want* doesn't fit in memory, **gradient accumulation** simulates it — run several smaller micro-batches, sum their gradients, and step the optimizer only once after the last, which is numerically equivalent to one large batch at the cost of extra forward/backward passes.

An **epoch** is one full pass over the training set. You train for many epochs, watching validation loss; too few and the model underfits, too many and it overfits (which is exactly what early stopping in 15.5 guards against). Epochs and batch size interact: with batch size $B$ and dataset of $N$ examples, one epoch is $N/B$ optimizer steps, so a bigger batch means fewer updates per epoch and usually more epochs to reach the same point.

**Mixed precision** is the standard speed lever on modern GPUs. Most operations run in 16-bit floating point (half the memory, much faster matrix math on tensor cores) while a 32-bit "master copy" of the weights is kept for the accumulation steps that need the precision. Because 16-bit numbers underflow easily, small gradients can vanish to zero; **loss scaling** multiplies the loss by a large constant before backprop to push gradients into representable range, then divides it back out before the update. The payoff is roughly 2–3× faster training at negligible accuracy cost — which is why it is on by default in most modern training stacks.

::: {.callout-tip}
When something diverges, change one knob at a time and start with the learning rate. The overwhelming majority of "my network won't train" problems are a too-high learning rate, a missing warmup, or a missing/incorrect normalization layer — not an exotic bug.
:::

## 15.8 — Key takeaways

- The deep loss surface is non-convex; the real enemies are **saddle points and ravines**, not local minima, and **flat minima generalize better** than sharp ones.
- Optimizers form a ladder: SGD → **momentum** (fix ravines) → **RMSprop** (per-parameter rates) → **Adam** (both, with zero-bias correction) → **AdamW** (decoupled weight decay). AdamW is the modern default.
- The **learning rate** is the most important hyperparameter; find a starting value with an **LR range test** (or sweep powers of ten), then use **cosine decay with warmup** — warmup is near-mandatory for transformers.
- **BatchNorm** normalizes over the batch (great for CNNs); **LayerNorm** normalizes over features per example (the choice for transformers and RNNs); the "internal covariate shift" story is historical, with loss-surface smoothing the better-supported explanation.
- Fight overfitting with **dropout** (inverted: rescale at train time, do nothing at test), **weight decay, data augmentation, and early stopping** — and make sure augmentations preserve the label.
- **Vanishing/exploding gradients** are tamed by ReLU, **residual connections** (the "+1" gradient path), normalization, good initialization, and **gradient clipping**.
- **Batch size** trades noise for parallelism (scale the learning rate with it, and use **gradient accumulation** to simulate big batches that don't fit in memory); **mixed precision** with loss scaling roughly halves training time for free.

## 15.9 — See also

- **Chapter 14 — Neural Network Fundamentals**: backpropagation, activation functions, and weight initialization that this chapter builds on.
- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning**: the gradients, chain rule, and linear algebra underneath every optimizer.
- **Chapter 03 — The Machine Learning Process**: train/validation/test splits and the bias–variance framing behind regularization and early stopping.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers**: where LayerNorm, warmup, and AdamW are the default training recipe.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection**: choosing learning rates, batch sizes, and stopping points with principled validation.
- **Chapter 43 — AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference**: mixed precision, quantization, and the hardware side of large-scale training.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 14 · 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals](ch14.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [16 · 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks →](ch16.html)
